Test governance is layered correctly (e2e → integration → unit → boundary-check), but the layers are not aligned on which architectural smells they guard: boundary-check.test.ts guards web-shared and messaging, web-server-viewer-boundary.test.js guards the server↔viewer seam, but no layer guards the use ↔ web-viewer loop. This gap should be filled before the project-status-polling zone is formally dissolved.

Rex package has three concurrent structural anti-patterns (layer inversion, circular zone dependency, satellite over-coupling) all within the same package. This clustering suggests the internal zone boundaries were defined by directory proximity rather than by semantic or dependency direction — a re-zoning of rex internals is warranted.

All detected circular zone dependencies are intra-package. No cross-package circular dependencies exist, confirming the gateway pattern and four-tier hierarchy are structurally enforced at the package boundary level.

packages/rex/src/core/fix.ts is inside the prd-fix-command satellite zone but lives in the core/ directory — satellite zone policy requires CLI-only content with domain logic in rex-prd-engine; this file should be relocated to rex-prd-engine or the satellite policy needs an explicit documented exception

No satellite zone has a lateral import to any other satellite — the star topology from all three satellites to rex-prd-engine means there is zero lateral coupling risk when planning absorption; each satellite can be absorbed independently without affecting the others

api.ts (viewer inbound gateway for sibling zones) has zero detectable consumers in the codebase. If no sibling zones import through api.ts, it provides documentation value only — either enforce it with a boundary-check.test.ts assertion or document that sibling zone access is currently uncontrolled.

Runtime call graph and import graph produce divergent coupling signals for bidirectional zone pairs. For governance decisions, both signals should be consulted: import-graph bidirectionality may reflect type-import inflation, while call-graph unidirectionality confirms the actual runtime dependency direction.

Static import analysis and call-graph analysis produce conflicting metrics for the crash zone. The monorepo should establish a single canonical source of truth for zone boundary enforcement (recommend: static import graph) and deprecate call-graph cohesion scores for zones with documented import-only cross-zone edges.

The project-status-polling dissolution into web-viewer is the single highest-ROI structural change available: it eliminates a dual-fragility zone, removes an undocumented bidirectional import loop, and reduces total zone count by one — all without requiring changes to any consumer outside web-viewer.

web-viewer and hench-agent-engine both achieve high cohesion (0.95 and 0.99 respectively) but through different mechanisms: hench achieves it through gateway concentration and sub-zone directories; web-viewer achieves it while maintaining 121 cross-zone import edges. Cohesion alone is insufficient as a health metric for hub zones — coupling must be weighted equally in assessments.

The three Rex satellite zones collectively represent 13 files, 3 dual-fragility warnings, and 95 total outgoing calls into rex-prd-engine — a single absorption PR targeting all three would reduce zone count by 3, eliminate all dual-fragility warnings in the Rex package, and add only 13 files to a 216-file zone whose cohesion can absorb them without measurable degradation

external.ts re-exports createRequestDedup from messaging but omits createFetchPipeline and createWSPipeline, which are imported directly by four viewer files. The gateway is incomplete: the two most-called pipeline factories bypass the documented boundary, creating an undetected leaky abstraction in the viewer's outbound import surface.

Asymmetric documentation of bidirectional zone loops (crash exception documented in CLAUDE.md; use ↔ web-viewer not documented) creates a governance gap where future contributors cannot distinguish intentional exceptions from unresolved debt. All bidirectional loops should be either documented as exceptions or tracked as active dissolution targets.

The rex layer inversion (rex-unit → rex-cli) is not a broad structural problem — it is a single concrete file: packages/rex/src/analyze/guided.ts importing `info` from cli/output.ts. The 2 static import edges attributed to this inversion both trace to this one file (guided.ts imports two symbols: `info`, and indirectly shares cli/output.ts with callers). Resolving this one file eliminates the entire rex-unit → rex-cli inversion finding.

The practice of placing re-export barrels in CLI-tier modules (cli/output.ts) for foundation-tier symbols creates a gravity well: domain modules are attracted to the convenience barrel and unknowingly cross a tier boundary. This pattern is currently contained to one file in rex, but its presence as a precedent makes proactive enforcement (e.g., a domain-isolation.test.js assertion blocking `from.*cli/output`) valuable before it spreads.

The web-server zone's 4 concrete imports from ../shared/index.js and the reported `web-server → web-viewer: 5` cross-zone count do not match — either one import edge is unaccounted for, or zone classification assigns some shared/ files to web-viewer rather than web. This is an analysis-pipeline discrepancy that affects governance accuracy: if shared/ files are counted as web-viewer, web-viewer's coupling and size metrics are inflated, and web-server appears to violate the no-runtime-import contract when it does not.

The hench-agent-engine's 1 runtime outgoing call-graph edge vs 2 static import edges to hench-safety-guard confirms that type-import erosion is already occurring in this zone pair: one edge is purely structural (type-only import), not behavioral. Auditing all bidirectional zone pairs for type-vs-value import composition would identify which apparent cycles are type-import artifacts vs genuine runtime coupling.

packages/hench/src/tools/files.ts imports simpleGlobMatch directly from ../guard/paths.js — the tools layer has a runtime import dependency on the security guard module. If guard/paths.js is refactored for security policy changes, file tool behavior changes with it. simpleGlobMatch is a pure utility with no guard-specific logic and should be relocated to a shared hench utilities module to decouple tool implementation from the security layer.

packages/hench/src/agent/lifecycle/loop.ts imports GuardRails directly from ../../guard/index.js — the core agent loop holds a direct compile-time dependency on the guard implementation class. This means the guard cannot be replaced with an alternate safety implementation or mocked in unit tests without modifying the core loop. The dependency direction should be inverted: loop.ts should accept a guard interface injected at construction, not import the concrete GuardRails class.

The web/src/schema/ directory is the only shared-types surface in the web package that is consumed by both the server tier (routes-features.ts) and the viewer tier (external.ts), yet it is not represented as a zone in web-shared or as its own zone. This creates a structural blind spot: changes to schema types have a cross-tier blast radius that zone coupling metrics do not surface.

The rex-cli zone (27+ files in packages/rex/src/cli/commands/) has a ZONE_BOUNDARY.md present but is not included in the analysis batch. With 27 flat-directory command files and high coupling to rex-prd-engine, it is the highest-file-count satellite zone in the rex package and the most likely source of future dual-fragility drift — its absence from governance analysis rounds means incremental additions will not trigger cohesion regression alerts until a full re-analysis.

use-facet-state.ts and tree-search.ts ended up in viewer-message-pipeline through a two-hop Louvain propagation: use-facet-state.ts was grouped with messaging due to polling-adjacent import patterns, then its import of SearchFacets from tree-search.ts pulled tree-search into the same zone. The misclassification cascades from a single boundary error — correcting use-facet-state.ts's zone pin removes both files without separate archetype overrides.

api.ts and external.ts are a declared symmetric gateway pair (external.ts for viewer→messaging outbound; api.ts for sibling zones→viewer inbound), but only external.ts appears in CLAUDE.md's gateway module registry table and only external.ts has enforcement via boundary-check.test.ts. The asymmetric documentation and enforcement mean api.ts is a paper boundary while external.ts is an active one — either enroll api.ts in the registry and add a boundary assertion, or explicitly document it as aspirational only.

Four files bypass external.ts for messaging imports, but they split into two architectural categories: hooks (use-prd-data.ts, use-prd-websocket.ts, use-project-status.ts) where direct pipeline factory imports are debatable infrastructure composition, and a component (execution-panel.ts) where direct pipeline construction is a clear separation-of-concerns violation. Enforcement strategies for the two categories differ: hooks could be exempted with documentation, but execution-panel.ts requires refactoring to delegate to a hook.

The overview dashboard (packages/web/src/viewer/views/overview.ts:120) counts 'zones needing attention' using OR logic (cohesion < 0.4 OR coupling > 0.5) with a cohesion threshold of 0.4 — but CLAUDE.md defines dual-fragility as BOTH thresholds simultaneously (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5). The dashboard therefore overcounts attention zones using a lower cohesion bar and looser boolean logic, making it an unreliable indicator of actual governance risk.

The bidirectional anti-pattern count in overview.ts (line 86–90) is computed by text-matching f.text.includes('Bidirectional') against finding strings. This creates an invisible coupling between finding serialization format and UI rendering logic: any change to finding text structure (capitalization, wording) silently zeros the antipattern badge without a compile-time or test-time error.

The web (messaging) zone's 15 imports into web-viewer likely include type dependencies (schema types, view IDs) that already have a valid home in web-shared. If those 15 edges are predominantly type imports rather than value imports, redirecting them to web-shared — which the messaging zone is already permitted to import directly per the messaging exemption — would eliminate the bidirectional loop structurally without dissolving or merging either zone.

packages/hench/src/process/ files (limiter.ts, memory-monitor.ts, memory-throttle.ts) import directly from hench-guard. This means the agent's process-management sub-zone has a mandatory compile-time dependency on the guard sub-zone, making it architecturally impossible to run the process layer without the guard present. The guard is nominally an optional safety wrapper, but these reverse-direction imports from process/ → guard make guard load-bearing inside the engine — a stronger coupling than the documented 'guard wraps agent' pattern implies.

The three Rex structural anti-patterns (layer inversion rex-unit → rex-cli, circular dependency rex-unit ↔ rex-core, satellite over-coupling fix/rex-core → rex-unit) are structurally independent failures. Fixing the circular dependency alone (e.g., by moving rex-core content into rex-unit) does not fix the layer inversion; fixing the layer inversion (moving the 2 rex-unit → rex-cli edges) does not resolve the satellites. A piecemeal approach risks introducing new violations while resolving existing ones — the three patterns require a coordinated re-zoning pass, not sequential individual fixes.

packages/rex/src/analyze/guided.ts is the only domain module in the monorepo that imports a logging primitive from a CLI-tier module (cli/output.ts) when the same primitive is available from the foundation tier (@n-dx/llm-client). This creates the sole concrete rex-unit → rex-cli layer inversion. The fix is a one-line import change; leaving it unresolved creates precedent for future domain modules to import from CLI tier.

Zone classification of src/shared/ files within the web package may be inconsistent between analysis passes, causing web-server → web-viewer cross-zone import counts to include what are actually web-server → web-shared edges. This inflates web-viewer's apparent inbound coupling metrics and makes the web-server composition root appear to violate its documented no-runtime-import contract. Zone pin verification for src/shared/ files is needed to establish accurate governance baselines.

hench tools/files.ts imports simpleGlobMatch from guard/paths.js, creating a hard compile-time dependency from the tools sub-zone into the security guard zone. The guard zone is supposed to wrap the agent (unidirectional), but this import inverts the dependency at the utility level — tools cannot be tested or deployed without the guard module present.

hench agent/lifecycle/loop.ts imports the concrete GuardRails class from guard/index.js. This couples the core agent loop to a specific guard implementation, preventing guard substitution (test doubles, alternate policies) without modifying loop.ts. The injection seam registry in CLAUDE.md documents callback injection as the preferred pattern for this type of cross-zone wiring — GuardRails should be injected rather than imported directly.

web/src/schema/ (shared type definitions used by both web-server and web-viewer) has no dedicated zone classification and no governance policy in CLAUDE.md. It is the only cross-tier shared surface in the web package without explicit boundary enforcement — adding a server-only import (e.g., a node: type) to schema/ would silently contaminate the viewer bundle at next build without triggering any boundary-check assertion.

api.ts is missing from CLAUDE.md's gateway module registry table (the table lists external.ts, hench/rex-gateway.ts, hench/llm-gateway.ts, web/rex-gateway.ts, web/domain-gateway.ts, but not web/viewer/api.ts). A contributor auditing the gateway surface sees no record of api.ts, creating an invisible enforcement gap for the viewer's inbound contract. Add api.ts to the table with its intended consumers (crash, route, performance zones) and an enforcement status note.

overview.ts:120 uses cohesion < 0.4 OR coupling > 0.5 to flag attention zones, but CLAUDE.md dual-fragility policy is cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5. The mismatch means the dashboard both overcounts (OR vs AND) and uses a misaligned cohesion threshold (0.4 vs 0.5), creating a misleading governance indicator visible to every dashboard consumer.

overview.ts detects bidirectional anti-patterns via f.text.includes('Bidirectional') string match. This is an undocumented coupling between finding serialization format and dashboard rendering; changes to finding text format silently break the antipattern count with no compile-time or test-time signal.

The overview.ts refactor introduced a divergence between what the dashboard renders (compact attentionCount summary) and what the CSS defines (full attention-item/issue-tag block layout). CSS and component code are now out of sync, creating a documentation hazard — future contributors reading overview.css will expect DOM structure that does not exist.

The ZoneMap component removal from overview.ts reduces the dashboard's visual architectural coverage — users can no longer see the zone graph from the overview page and must navigate to the zones view. If this was intentional, document the navigation path in the overview UI; if unintentional, the ZoneMap component should be restored before the export becomes stale.

riskMetrics removal from zones.json and retention in v1.ts schema creates a schema drift pattern: the type says the field may exist, the data says it never does. This kind of optional-but-absent pattern accumulates over time and erodes schema trust — a follow-up pass should either remove the type field or restore the data, not leave both in an inconsistent state.

overview.ts:198 applies both 'enrichment-badge' and 'reset-badge' CSS classes to the post-reset indicator, but overview.css defines no .reset-badge rule — the badge renders identically to a normal enrichment badge, making it visually impossible to distinguish a reset event from an incremental pass

The 'green' cohesion threshold diverges across three viewer files: architecture.ts:49 uses cohesion ≥ 0.6, while overview.ts:254 and zone-map.ts:125 both use cohesion ≥ 0.7. A zone with cohesion 0.65 displays as healthy (green) on the architecture view and degraded (orange) on the overview and zone-map views simultaneously — three views cannot agree on the same zone's health status

faq.ts:43 documents coupling > 0.6 as the threshold for suggesting a zone merge, but overview.ts:120 flags zones for attention at coupling > 0.5. The monorepo now has four distinct cohesion threshold values in active production code: 0.4 (orange floor in overview/architecture/zone-map), 0.5 (CLAUDE.md dual-fragility warning threshold), 0.6 (green floor in architecture.ts), 0.7 (green floor in overview.ts and zone-map.ts). Four thresholds for a single concept with no shared constant means any future threshold adjustment requires hunting four separate files

The overview dashboard has three independent cohesion threshold values (0.4 for attentionCount, 0.4/0.7 for health dots, 0.5 in CLAUDE.md governance) — this means the 'zones needing attention' count, the per-zone health indicator, and the written governance policy will never agree on which zones are at risk, making the dashboard an unreliable signal for the documented dual-fragility governance process

overview.css contains orphaned CSS rules (.attention-list, .attention-item, .insight-item, etc.) for UI components that no longer exist in overview.ts — this is evidence that a UI iteration removed rendered sections without cleaning up their styles, a pattern that will recur unless CSS removal is part of the component-change review checklist

The health dot color calculation in overview.ts is cohesion-only while CLAUDE.md's governance definition is dual-axis (cohesion AND coupling). This creates a systematic blind spot: any zone that is healthy on cohesion but fragile on coupling will appear green in the dashboard while simultaneously meeting the dual-fragility criterion in the governance policy — the dashboard actively contradicts the written governance definition for this class of zones

overview.ts:254-256 computes the Top Zones health-dot color using cohesion alone (>= 0.7 green, >= 0.4 orange, else red) with no coupling component. A zone with adequate cohesion (e.g. 0.7) but very high coupling (e.g. 0.9) receives a green dot despite representing a significant structural risk. The health-dot is therefore silent on the coupling half of the dual-fragility definition.

overview.ts applies two distinct cohesion thresholds within the same file: 0.4 for the attentionCount badge and 0.7/0.4 for healthColor in Top Zones. Neither matches the CLAUDE.md dual-fragility threshold of 0.5. Three threshold definitions for the same governance concept exist across two locations with no shared constant, making future realignment require coordinated edits in multiple places with no automated consistency check.

overview.css defines seven CSS classes — .attention-list, .attention-item, .attention-name, .attention-issues, .issue-tag, .insight-item, .insight-more — that have no usages in any production component. responsive.css also carries orphaned overrides for .attention-item and .attention-issues. These appear to be remnants of a removed 'zones needing attention' detail list that was replaced by the simpler zone-attention-note badge; the dead rules add maintenance noise and inflate the CSS surface.

overview.ts:199 applies class 'enrichment-badge reset-badge' to the lastReset indicator, but reset-badge has no CSS definition anywhere in the codebase. The reset badge renders identically to a regular enrichment badge with no visual differentiation — a silent UI regression where the reset state is visually indistinguishable from an enrichment pass badge.

overview.ts (the primary governance dashboard view) has zero unit tests. The three threshold-dependent governance indicators — attentionCount (boolean logic + threshold), healthColor (cohesion-only color bands), and bidirectional antipattern count (text.includes string match) — are all untested. Bugs in these calculations produce misleading governance signals without any compile-time or test-time detection.

overview.css and overview.ts are now structurally misaligned: the CSS defines layout for attention-item, issue-tag, and insight-item blocks that the component no longer renders. Establish a pattern of co-locating CSS with its component (or using CSS-in-JS / scoped styles) to make dead style detection mechanical rather than requiring manual cross-file audits.

The ZoneRiskMetrics type should be deprecated or removed from sourcevision/src/schema/v1.ts in a follow-up PR now that riskMetrics has been removed from all zone data. Leaving an optional type field for data that is never populated erodes schema reliability and will silently mislead future consumers who write code against the type.

overview.ts:198 applies CSS class 'reset-badge' alongside 'enrichment-badge' for the post-reset state indicator, but overview.css contains no .reset-badge rule. The badge is visually indistinguishable from a normal enrichment badge. Add a distinct .reset-badge style (e.g., different background color or border) so users can identify when a reset has occurred.

The 'green' (healthy) cohesion threshold is 0.6 in architecture.ts:49 but 0.7 in overview.ts:254 and zone-map.ts:125. The same zone can simultaneously appear green on the architecture view and orange on the overview and zone-map views. Extract a single ZONE_HEALTH_GREEN_THRESHOLD constant into web-shared (or a viewer constants file) and import it in all three view files to eliminate the cross-view contradiction.

faq.ts:43 hardcodes coupling > 0.6 as the high-coupling threshold ('high coupling (>0.6) suggests merging'), but overview.ts:120 flags attention zones at coupling > 0.5. A user reading the FAQ will not understand why their zone with coupling 0.55 is flagged by the UI. Align the FAQ text to the actual attention threshold (0.5), or extract the threshold to a named constant that the FAQ text and the attention logic both reference.

Four distinct cohesion threshold values are scattered across governance docs and viewer code: 0.4 (orange floor), 0.5 (CLAUDE.md dual-fragility), 0.6 (architecture.ts green floor), 0.7 (overview.ts/zone-map.ts green floor). Consolidate to two named canonical thresholds — COHESION_WARN (0.5, aligning with CLAUDE.md) and COHESION_CRITICAL (0.4) — defined in a single location and imported by both governance tooling and viewer components.

CSS orphan prevention: when removing a rendered section from a Preact component, the corresponding CSS class definitions must be removed in the same PR. The current state (7 orphaned classes in overview.css) suggests this step is not on the component-change review checklist. Adding a CSS-unused-class lint rule or a reviewer checklist item would prevent accumulation of dead style rules.

Governance threshold synchronization: CLAUDE.md defines dual-fragility as cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5. The dashboard (overview.ts:120, 254) uses different thresholds (0.4 and 0.7) inconsistently. Introduce a shared constant (e.g., DUAL_FRAGILITY_COHESION_THRESHOLD = 0.5, DUAL_FRAGILITY_COUPLING_THRESHOLD = 0.5) imported by both the dashboard and any test assertions to ensure all three signals remain synchronized when governance thresholds change.

Extract the dual-fragility thresholds (cohesion < 0.5, coupling > 0.5) into named constants shared by overview.ts and any future governance-reporting code. Currently overview.ts hard-codes 0.4 (attentionCount) and 0.7/0.4 (healthColor) in two places that do not match each other or CLAUDE.md policy — a single constant would make threshold alignment mechanical rather than requiring human coordination.

Remove the seven dead CSS rules in overview.css (.attention-list, .attention-item, .attention-name, .attention-issues, .issue-tag, .insight-item, .insight-more) and their responsive.css overrides. These classes have no production consumers and appear to be remnants of a removed attention-detail list section.

Define a CSS rule for .reset-badge in overview.css to visually differentiate the enrichment reset badge from a normal enrichment pass badge. Currently the two states are rendered identically because reset-badge has no styles, defeating the purpose of showing reset information separately.

Update the Top Zones health-dot color logic in overview.ts to incorporate coupling in addition to cohesion — for example, using red when cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5 (dual-fragility), orange when either threshold is crossed individually, and green only when both are healthy. The current cohesion-only calculation makes the health dot blind to high-coupling zones.
